text: The penalty phase of the trial of Boston Marathon bomber, Dzhokhar Tsarnaev, has begun with the prosecution arguing for the death penalty due to the "heinous, cruel and depraved" nature of the crime. Tsarnaev placed a bomb in a crowd, killing three people and injuring 260 others. The prosecution has highlighted the substantial planning and premeditation involved in the attack, as well as Tsarnaev's apparent lack of remorse. The defence is expected to argue that Dzhokhar was influenced by his older brother, Tamerlan, and was not fully responsible for his actions. They may also argue that at 19, Dzhokhar's brain was not fully developed, and therefore he could not be held fully responsible. The jury will need to reach a unanimous decision for Tsarnaev to receive the death penalty. The author predicts a 10-2 or 9-3 vote in favour of death, but expects that the historical record will proclaim that the jury chose life for Tsarnaev.

The article discusses the ongoing trial of Dzhokhar Tsarnaev, the surviving Boston Marathon bomber, and the question of what he deserves as punishment. The prosecution has emphasized the heinous and cruel nature of the crime, with Tsarnaev placing a bomb in a crowd and causing the death of three people and injuries to 260 others, including children. The prosecution also highlights the substantial planning and premeditation involved, as well as the betrayal of the United States, the country that gave Tsarnaev shelter and citizenship. The defense, on the other hand, argues that Tsarnaev was influenced by his older brother and was not fully responsible for his actions, citing brain development research that suggests individuals under 25 are not fully mature. However, the article questions the connection between brain development and moral responsibility, comparing it to the celebration of bravery and heroism in young adults. The author predicts that the jury will vote in favor of the death penalty, but notes that a unanimous decision is required. The article concludes by discussing the advantages the defense has in the trial and the need for the punishment to fit the crime, arguing that Tsarnaev is among the worst of the worst.

text: Jurors from the trial of former NFL star Aaron Hernandez have given their first nationally televised interview, in which they described the emotional toll of the trial and their shock at the defendant's "indifference" to the killing of Odin Lloyd. The jury had deliberated for more than 35 hours over seven days, hearing the testimony of over 130 witnesses and reviewing more than 400 pieces of evidence. "It's been an incredibly emotional toll on all of us," Lesa Strachan, the jury foreperson, told CNN's Anderson Cooper. Juror Kelly Dorsey said that the fact Hernandez was a football player did not affect her vote to convict, nor did it matter whether he actually pulled the trigger. "To leave your friend on the ground, knowing that he's not there anymore -- he's either dead or he's going to die -- that's indifference," she said. The word "indifference" was used multiple times by members of the jury. Hernandez, once a $40 million pro-football player, was sentenced to life without parole.

text: The jury in the Massachusetts murder trial of former NFL star Aaron Hernandez has spoken out about their emotional toll during the trial. After more than 35 hours of deliberation, the jury found Hernandez guilty of first-degree murder in the shooting death of Odin Lloyd. Lesa Strachan, the jury foreperson, expressed her shock at the viciousness of the crime, noting that Lloyd was shot six times. The jury members emphasized that Hernandez being a football player did not influence their decision to convict him, and they were all equal in the eyes of the law. They were also surprised to receive congratulatory calls after the verdict, as they felt there were no winners in this case. The jurors declined to discuss the dynamics within the jury room, but stressed the seriousness and gravity of their task. Hernandez, who went from a pro-football contract to a life sentence without parole, will now face prison life at a maximum-security facility. The jury members expressed their sadness for Lloyd's family, especially his young daughter who will never see her father again.

text: After 35 hours of deliberation over a seven-day period, the jury in the Aaron Hernandez murder trial convicted the former NFL star of first-degree murder in the 2013 shooting death of his onetime friend, Odin Lloyd. The jury was composed of 12 men and women, including a woman who had never heard of Hernandez before the trial. The verdict was based on testimony from more than 130 witnesses and evidence from over 400 pieces. The jury foreperson, Lesa Strachan, said she was struck by the viciousness of the multiple gunshots and that there was no need for such violence. Juror Kelly Dorsey, who watched the Patriots every Sunday during the football season, said she wasn't affected by Hernandez's celebrity status and that he played football for a living didn't influence her vote. Rosalie Oliver, another juror, said she was surprised to hear from friends congratulating her after the verdict and that the worst part of the trial was the thought of the little girl who would never see her father again. The jury declined to discuss the dynamics inside the jury room, focusing instead on the evidence presented during the trial.

text: After deliberating for 35 hours over 7 days, a jury has convicted former NFL star Aaron Hernandez of first-degree murder in the 2013 shooting death of his onetime friend Odin Lloyd. The jury, consisting of 12 men and women, was struck by the viciousness of multiple gunshots and the lack of empathy shown by Hernandez. Despite being a well-known football player, the jurors emphasized that his celebrity status did not influence their decision. They were also surprised by the calls they received from friends congratulating them after the verdict, and expressed the importance of treating all individuals equally under the law. The jurors declined to discuss the dynamics inside the jury room, focusing instead on the evidence presented during the trial. Hernandez faces a sentence of life in prison without parole.
